original_abstract: The dynamic restructuring of copper under electrochemical conditions is widely suspected to be the origin of its unique reactivity toward CO₂ electroreduction. Surface roughening—characterized by the formation of atomic vacancies and adatoms—is a central mechanism for this activation; yet, the precise atomic structure of these motifs under realistic operating conditions remains poorly understood. In this study, we explore the chemical landscape of Cu surface restructuring under varying CO and H coverage by integrating grand canonical density functional theory (DFT) with global optimization techniques, allowing us to construct a potential-dependent grand canonical ensemble. Our results indicate that while intermediate regimes of mixed CO/H coverage—which host elevated Cu surface atoms—are thermodynamically unfavorable, they are kinetically inevitable during operation. To capture this behavior, we developed a quasi-kinetic Monte Carlo simulation to track the system's evolution during a cathodic scan, identifying specific metastable structures that emerge along the reaction path. Chemical bonding analysis of these metastable Cu*CO species elucidates their formation mechanisms, while molecular dynamics and free energy calculations reveal the pathways through which these species diffuse and dimerize. This work provides deep atomistic insights into the phenomenon of surface roughening and offers a generalizable framework for simulating the nonequilibrium evolution of restructuring surfaces under mixed-adsorbate conditions.
humanized_abstract: Copper’s ability to activate and catalyze the CO2 reduction reaction (RR) remains one of the greatest mysteries in the field of electrocatalysis. While the effect of surface roughening due to atomic vacancies and adatoms is widely accepted, the exact structure of the active sites remains unclear. Herein, we elucidate the Cu surface structure as a function of potential and varying CO and H coverage through the integration of density functional theory (DFT) and global optimization methods to form a potential-dependent grand canonical ensemble. While the metastable mixed CO/H coverage regime, which has the highest Cu surface atom concentration, is unfavorable, it is unavoidable during the operation of the electrode. A quasi-kinetic Monte Carlo model is used to elucidate the dynamic behavior of the surface during a cathodic scan. The metastable Cu*CO structures are identified along the reaction pathway. Their bonding and mechanism of formation are elucidated by an in-depth analysis of the chemical bonding in these structures. Molecular dynamics and free energy calculations are employed to elucidate the diffusion and dimerization pathways of the Cu*CO structures. This study not only elucidates the Cu surface structure under electrochemical conditions but also introduces a methodology to model the nonequilibrium behavior of the surface restructuring under mixed adsorbate conditions.
